results.possibleIdentity: Robert Kragen (born 1945) is an artist and technologist who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, particularly within the field of early video art and kinetic installations. He is notably associated with the non-profit organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg. Kragen frequently collaborated with Robert Lippman and Irv Rosner as part of a collective (often referred to as Kragen, Lippman & Rosner). His work in 1967 and 1968 involved using the television set as a medium for creating abstract images, following the experimental tradition of Nam June Paik. Under the name Morning Light Co., he and his collaborators contributed to major E.A.T. projects, including the design and development of the Pepsi-Cola Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. His artistic practice bridged the gap between traditional sculpture and emerging television and light technologies.

results.possibleIdentity: Roy M. Steinberg (1929–2016) was an American Modernist painter and educator who was most active and prolific during the 1960s and 1970s. Born in Bartow, Florida, he served in the United States Navy during the Korean War before using the GI Bill to earn a BFA from the University of Florida in 1952. In 1956, he moved to New York City to study at the Art Students League for six years, eventually becoming a Life Member. Steinberg's work is characterized by a blend of abstract expressionism and representational form. He established a studio at the Barron Arts Center in Woodbridge, New Jersey, where he taught figure drawing and sketching. His career included solo exhibitions at the Wrans Gallery in New Jersey and the Celedon Gallery in SoHo, and his paintings are held in collections such as the Mellon Collection, the Atlantic County Museum, and the Beaux Arts Gallery in Fort Lauderdale. In his later years, he co-hosted the radio series 'Festival of the Arts' on WPBR in Florida. He was married to fellow artist Gail Steinberg and remained active in the art community until the early 2000s.

results.possibleIdentity: John Harris (born 1937) is an American artist known for his collaboration with Fred Stern during the late 1960s and early 1970s, particularly in projects associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He is most recognized for the work 'K.E.P.-1' (Kinetic Environment Project), created in 1968 in partnership with Stern. This project was featured in the landmark E.A.T. exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' held at the Brooklyn Museum from November 1968 to January 1969. His work during this period often involved the intersection of art, engineering, and technology, consistent with the mission of E.A.T. to foster collaborations between artists and scientists. Documentation of his installation and collaborative efforts is preserved in collections such as those at the Getty Research Institute.

John Harris (born 1948) is a prominent British painter and illustrator who began his professional career in the early 1970s after graduating from the Exeter College of Art in 1970. Although primarily known for his later work in science fiction and 'imaginative realism,' his early career in the late 1960s and 1970s involved a period of study in transcendental meditation which heavily influenced his artistic focus on monumental scale and space. In 1984, he was commissioned by NASA to document a Space Shuttle launch, becoming the first British artist to be so honored. His work frequently explores the relationship between technology (specifically spacecraft and futuristic structures) and vast landscapes, which aligns with themes of art and science collaboration, though his direct involvement with the E.A.T. organization is less documented than the American artist of the same name.

results.possibleIdentity: Nam June Paik (1932–2006) was a South Korean-born American artist widely celebrated as the "father of video art." He was highly active during the late 1960s and early 1970s, a period when he revolutionized the use of television and electronic media in art. After moving to New York in 1964, he became a key figure in the Fluxus movement and collaborated extensively with cellist Charlotte Moorman and electronics engineer Shuya Abe. Paik was also a participant in the pioneering activities of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), notably involved in the 1966 "9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering" performances that served as a foundational moment for the organization. His landmark works from this era include "Exposition of Music – Electronic Television" (1963), the remote-controlled "Robot K-456" (1964), and "TV Bra for Living Sculpture" (1969). In 1974, he famously coined the term "electronic superhighway" to describe the future of global telecommunications.

results.possibleIdentity: Phyllis Gershuny (now known as Phyllis Segura) is an artist and editor who was a pioneering figure in the early video art movement of the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is best known as the co-founder and original co-editor of Radical Software, the first periodical dedicated to independent video art and alternate information structures, which she launched in 1970 alongside Beryl Korot and Ira Schneider. Before founding the magazine, Gershuny lived in Europe where she worked with the Living Theater and was introduced to video by British photographer John Hopkins. Upon returning to New York, she collaborated with the Raindance Corporation to publish Radical Software, though she was never formally invited to join the collective and left the publication after its third issue due to creative and organizational differences. Her work during this period was closely aligned with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), and she was active in the New York scene when E.A.T. was based at Automation House, where she organized early public-access cable screenings in bars. Gershuny eventually returned to painting and moved to the Hudson Valley, where she became a personal chef, a culinary radio personality hosting 'Eating It in the Hudson Valley,' and the founder of the poetry magazine Broadshirt. Her historical contributions have been featured in major retrospectives, such as the 2024–2025 exhibition 'Radical Software: Women, Art & Computing 1960–1991.'

results.possibleIdentity: Susan Alexander is a contemporary dancer, choreographer, and educator who was part of the Merce Cunningham circle in New York during the late 1960s and 1970s. She is associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) community, likely through her extensive work as a faculty member and assistant at the Merce Cunningham Dance Studio. Merce Cunningham was one of the co-founders of E.A.T. along with Robert Rauschenberg and Billy Klüver. Alexander led a residency at UC Berkeley in the late 1960s and has taught at the Paris Conservatory of Music and Dance and the Paris Opera Ballet. Her artistic practice has included collaborations with filmmakers, notably with David Robinson on the project "Moving Bodies/Moving Pictures," which received support from the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA). Historical records of E.A.T. activities and dance techniques from the 1960s often list her alongside other key collaborators of the era, such as Ruth Barnes and Merce Cunningham.

Susan Alexander (also known as Susan Alexjander) is a composer and sound artist known for her interdisciplinary collaborations between art and science. She is particularly recognized for her work "Sequencia" (1990), where she collaborated with biologist David Deamer to translate the vibrational frequencies of DNA base molecules into audible sound using synthesizers. While her most prominent work emerged in the late 1980s and 1990s, she is cited in historical reports (such as those by the Smithsonian) alongside the legacy of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) due to her focus on the intersection of artistic expression and scientific data. She holds a Master's degree in Composition and Theory from San Jose State University and has had her works featured in major science and art institutions, including the Boston Museum of Science and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art.

results.possibleIdentity: Ji Hurwitz is most likely a reference to Jacob Isaac Hurwitz (often cited as J. I. Hurwitz or JI Hurwitz), a social psychologist who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his foundational research on group dynamics, specifically his paper 'Some Effects of Power on the Relations among Group Members' (1953, often reprinted in the 1960s and 70s). While Hurwitz was not primarily an artist, his work focused on how individuals of different statuses communicate and interact within small groups, a subject of significant interest to organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). E.A.T. was a non-profit organization that facilitated collaborations between artists, engineers, and scientists, often focusing on the social processes and dynamics of multidisciplinary teamwork. Hurwitz's work on status and communication in groups is frequently cited in academic and technical literature of that era concerning social engineering and collaborative structures.
